questions.question: Define queue data structure and explain the FIFO principle with a real-world example.
keyPoints: Queue is a linear data structure following FIFO (First In, First Out)
Elements are added at rear and removed from front
Real-world example: People standing in ticket counter queue or bus stop queue
The first person in line is the first to get service

questions.question: Explain the terms overflow and underflow in context of queue operations.
keyPoints: Overflow: Condition when queue is full and no more elements can be added
Underflow: Condition when queue is empty and no elements can be removed
Overflow occurs during enqueue when rear reaches maximum size
Underflow occurs during dequeue when front exceeds rear

questions.question: Write pseudo-code for enqueue and dequeue operations in an array-based queue.
keyPoints: Enqueue: Check overflow, increment rear, add element at rear position
Dequeue: Check underflow, store front element, increment front, return element
Maintain front and rear pointers
Initialize front and rear appropriately

questions.question: What are the advantages of circular queue over linear queue?
keyPoints: Circular queue efficiently reuses empty spaces
Eliminates phantom overflow problem of linear queue
Both front and rear pointers can wrap around
Maximum capacity can be fully utilized

questions.question: List at least four applications of queue data structure in computing.
keyPoints: CPU task scheduling in operating systems
Print job spooling in printer management
Breadth-First Search (BFS) in graphs
Asynchronous data transfer between processes
Web server request handling
Call center customer service systems

questions.question: Explain the difference between linear queue and priority queue.
keyPoints: Linear queue: Elements follow FIFO order
Priority queue: Elements removed based on priority, not arrival order
In priority queue, higher priority elements dequeue first
Linear queue maintains strict insertion order

questions.question: Describe queue implementation using linked list with necessary data structures.
keyPoints: Use two pointers: front and rear pointing to chain nodes
Each node contains data and pointer to next node
Enqueue: Create new node, attach to rear, update rear pointer
Dequeue: Remove node from front, update front pointer
Dynamic size allocation, no overflow (except system memory)

questions.question: Explain array representation of a queue with diagrams. Write pseudo-code for enqueue and dequeue operations, considering overflow and underflow conditions.
keyPoints: Array-based queue uses one-dimensional array with front and rear indices
Initialize front = -1, rear = -1 for empty queue
Enqueue: Check rear == MAXSIZE - 1 for overflow, increment rear, insert element
Dequeue: Check front == -1 or front > rear for underflow, retrieve element at front, increment front
Trace examples showing different operations

questions.question: Explain circular queue with array implementation. How does it overcome the limitations of linear queue? Write pseudo-code for enqueue and dequeue in circular queue.
keyPoints: Circular queue connects rear to front using modular arithmetic
Eliminates wasted space problem of linear queue
Enqueue: Use (rear + 1) % MAXSIZE to calculate new rear position
Dequeue: Use (front + 1) % MAXSIZE to calculate new front position
Check condition (rear + 1) % MAXSIZE == front for full queue
Solves phantom overflow by reusing vacant positions

questions.question: Write a C program to implement queue using array with operations: enqueue, dequeue, display, isEmpty, isFull.
keyPoints: Define MAXSIZE and array for queue storage
Maintain front and rear variables
Implement isFull: rear == MAXSIZE - 1
Implement isEmpty: front == -1 or front > rear
Enqueue: Increment rear, store element at rear index
Dequeue: Return element at front, increment front
Display: Loop from front to rear

questions.question: Explain the working of Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm using queue data structure with an example graph.
keyPoints: BFS explores vertices level by level
Start from source vertex, mark visited, enqueue source
While queue not empty: dequeue vertex, visit all unvisited neighbors, enqueue them
Continue until all reachable vertices are visited
Example: Tree or graph traversal showing level-wise order
Time complexity: O(V + E) where V=vertices, E=edges
